event: Early humans, including Neanderthals, leave evidence of habitation at La Cotte de St Brelade, one of the most significant Palaeolithic sites in northwestern Europe.
Rising sea levels at the end of the last Ice Age sever Jersey from mainland Europe, transforming the peninsula into an island.
Neolithic farmers settle Jersey and begin constructing megalithic passage graves and dolmens such as La Hougue Bie.
Bronze Age communities flourish on the island, leaving behind hoards, tools, and burial sites.
Celtic Iron Age tribes inhabit Jersey, with Armorican coin hoards like Le Catillon attesting to trade and refuge during the Roman conquest of Gaul.
Jersey lies within the orbit of Roman Gaul, with Roman-era artefacts and coinage circulating on the island.
Christianity spreads to the Channel Islands, with traditions linking St Helier, a hermit martyr, to the evangelisation of Jersey.
The Channel Islands, including Jersey, are annexed to the Duchy of Normandy under Duke William Longsword.
Duke William of Normandy conquers England, uniting Jersey's Norman rulers with the English crown.
King John loses mainland Normandy to France, but Jersey remains loyal to the English crown, beginning its status as a self-governing crown dependency.
The Treaty of Paris confirms English (Plantagenet) sovereignty over the Channel Islands while France retains continental Normandy.
French forces raid and ravage Jersey during the Anglo-French wars, prompting greater investment in island defences.
Royal commissioners' inquiries help formalise Jersey's distinct laws, customs, and the office of the Bailiff.
Bertrand du Guesclin besieges Mont Orgueil Castle during a French invasion, but the island is retained for the English crown.
French forces occupy Jersey during the Wars of the Roses, holding parts of the island for several years.
Sir Richard Harliston recaptures Mont Orgueil, ending the French occupation and restoring English control.
A papal bull confirms the Privilege of Neutrality, exempting the Channel Islands from Anglo-French hostilities and aiding island commerce.
Jersey adopts the Protestant Reformation, with Calvinist influence shaping the island's churches and society.
Construction begins on Elizabeth Castle in St Aubin's Bay, named in honour of Queen Elizabeth I and built to counter advances in artillery.
An Order in Council settles a long dispute by separating civil and military authority, defining the roles of the Bailiff and the Governor.
Charles, Prince of Wales, takes refuge in Jersey during the English Civil War as the island declares for the Royalist cause.
Parliamentary forces under Admiral Blake capture Jersey, ending Royalist resistance in the Channel Islands.
Huguenot refugees fleeing France after the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes settle in Jersey, reinforcing its Protestant character.
Popular unrest over grain shortages and corn exports leads to reforms, including the Code of 1771 codifying Jersey law.
The Battle of Jersey sees Major Peirson defeat a French invasion force in St Helier, the last serious attempt to seize the island.
Construction of coastal Martello towers and fortifications continues to defend Jersey during the Napoleonic Wars.
Jersey's shipbuilding and oyster-fishing industries boom, alongside expanding trade with North America and the cod fisheries.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert visit Jersey, and a new harbour at St Helier is developed to support growing commerce.
The Jersey cattle breed gains worldwide fame, and the island's agriculture shifts toward dairy and early potato exports.
Jersey adopts measures to protect the purity of its renowned Jersey cattle by restricting the import of foreign cattle.
Jersey's Royal (New Potato) becomes a celebrated export crop, anchoring the island's agricultural economy.
Reforms to the States of Jersey introduce elected Deputies, broadening the island's representative government.
Women gain the right to vote and stand in Jersey elections following the upheavals of the First World War.
German forces occupy Jersey after Britain demilitarises the Channel Islands, beginning nearly five years of Nazi occupation.
Occupying German forces deport over a thousand Jersey residents to internment camps in Germany.
After the Allied D-Day landings cut off supplies, the Red Cross ship SS Vega brings relief parcels to a starving population.
Jersey is liberated on 9 May, now celebrated annually as Liberation Day, ending the German occupation.
Major constitutional reform modernises the States of Jersey, removing the Rectors and Jurats from the legislature.
Jersey's English replaces Jersey French (Jèrriais) as the dominant language, though the Norman dialect persists in the countryside.
Jersey establishes itself as a low-tax finance centre, with banking and offshore services beginning to transform the economy.
When the United Kingdom joins the European Economic Community, Jersey gains a special relationship under Protocol 3 without full membership.
Jersey marks the bicentenary of the Battle of Jersey, reaffirming its distinct identity within the British Isles.
Jersey's finance industry matures into a leading international banking and fund-management centre.
Jersey reforms its government, creating a ministerial system led by a Chief Minister to replace the old committee structure.
Jersey faces international pressure over transparency and tax practices, prompting reforms to its offshore financial regulation.
Investigations into historic abuse at the Haut de la Garenne children's home draw national and international attention.
Following the UK's Brexit referendum, Jersey negotiates new customs and fishing arrangements outside the European Union.
A dispute over post-Brexit fishing licences leads French boats to blockade St Helier, prompting Royal Navy patrols off Jersey.
